lines.text: Against Me! — 'Sink, Florida, Sink.' 2007. New Wave. This one hits different at {{ELAPSED}} in.

Laura Jane Grace wrote this when she was still finding her way. Naples to Gainesville. That move — that's when everything changed. Sometimes you gotta leave the place you're from to understand why you hate it.
The whole world is against me — that's where the name came from. And by the time New Wave hit, she'd built something real. James Bowman, Andrew Seward, Atom Willard. A machine.
Number 57 on the Billboard 200. Not huge, but it was theirs. No compromises. Temperature's dropping out there, folks. Wind's kicking. This song feels right at 4 in the morning.
The Bouncing Souls. 'Ole.' 1997. Their self-titled Epitaph debut. This is the one — this is the singalong that kept people alive.
Greg Attonito, Pete Steinkopf, Bryan Kienlen — all three met in Basking Ridge High School. They moved to New Brunswick, and they decided: we're gonna build something together. No ego. Just community.
The Souls named themselves after Doc Martens advertising. 'Bouncing soles.' I love that — punk rock pulled straight from shoe commercials. Only in the '90s could that happen.
They called their fans 'True Believers.' Not customers. Not followers. Believers. That's the difference between a band and a religion, and the Souls understood it.
Play this one. Sing it. You're not alone tonight — not while this is playing.
Hot Water Music. 'Trusty Chords.' 1996. From Fuel for the Hate Game. Chuck Ragan and Chris Wollard sharing the mic like they're arguing in your living room.
Four kids in Gainesville, Florida — October 1994, they formed. Chuck, Chris, Jason Black, George Rebelo. The band's name came from a Bukowski short-story collection. That's literary punk, my friends.
They were so exhausted from touring that they nearly broke up in '98. But during their farewell show, something shifted. They wrote four new songs during rehearsal. Friendship saved the band.
The dual vocals — that call-and-response — it's like two parts of the same broken thing trying to make sense of staying together. I get it. We all get it at 04:00 on Purge night.
The Lawrence Arms. 'Coming Home.' 2002. Apathy and Exhaustion. Brendan Kelly and Chris McCaughan, Chicago guys, singing about what it means to go back.
They named themselves after the apartment they got evicted from. Middle of the night. No warning. So they turned it into art. That's punk.
Kelly said something real once: we have a lot of experiences — good and terrible ones — that are influenced by alcohol. That's what we write about. This song knows what that means.
They got kicked off Warped Tour in 2002 for calling out the five-dollar water bottles. Five dollars! For water! During Purge night, we understand institutional price-gouging real intimate-like.
Coming home means you left. And you're still not sure if leaving was worth it. Play this one sad.

tracks.title: Sunday Morning
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes. 'Sunday Morning.' 1997. From Have a Ball. These guys are a supergroup cover band, and they do what they want.
Spike Slawson, C.J. Ramone, Joey Cape — all these ringers came together to trash songs from other genres and remake them punk. Marty Feldman energy. Pure chaos and joy.
They recorded at One Week Records in San Francisco. Ryan Greene producing. The whole thing was: let's not take this seriously, let's make it ours, let's make it bleed.
They got booed off at PNC Park in 2006 during a Pirates game postgame Skyblast. Fireworks behind them. Crowd of thousands. And people still booed. That's strength — that's punk.
Sunday morning at 04:00 in the Purge. Time doesn't mean anything anymore.
NOFX. 'The Decline.' 1999. Eighteen minutes. One track. The longest 'fuck you' ever recorded to everything.
Fat Mike, Erik Sandin, Eric Melvin, El Hefe — these guys formed in 1983 at Fairfax High School in Los Angeles. They've been burning it down for 40 years. Forty. Years.
They never signed to a major. Never went corporate. Fat Wreck Chords is theirs. They control it. That's the whole point — the machine doesn't own you. You own you.
The Decline is social commentary wrapped in sarcasm wrapped in fury. It's cynical and it's right. By 1999, they knew what was coming. They tried to warn us.

Eighteen minutes of escalating rage. Temperature's dropping outside. Wind's picking up hard. This song is the sound of Madison at 04:00 AM on Purge night — the sound of things falling apart and nobody stopping it.
Sit with this one. Let it end you.

tickerItems.text: Madison cumulative: 165 (12 kills in the last hour). East Side still leading. Pre-dawn surge pattern tracking consistent with 2168.
Temperature 22°F and dropping. Wind NW 8 mph. Fog lifting off Lake Mendota. Visibility improving. Hard frost setting in.
Tim: All routes clear from Beltline to Capitol corridor. Wind gusts may affect visibility near the lake. Sunrise {{CLOCK}}. Two hours fifty-six minutes to all-clear.
